biomcp_log.q: HRV biofeedback slow breathing resonance frequency RCT meta-analysis
cognitive behavioral therapy insomnia CBT-I sleep efficiency RCT
grappling submission wrestling shoulder injury alternative martial arts
zone 2 training masters athletes VO2max preservation aging
heart rate variability biofeedback paced breathing 6 breaths per minute autonomic
resistance training sleep quality middle aged adults randomized
social connection physical activity group exercise wellbeing meta-analysis
sauna Finnish cardiovascular mortality longevity cohort
judo wrestling skill acquisition novelty cognitive benefit adult
evening light exposure melatonin sleep onset latency RCT
strength training mortality all-cause adults dose response meta-analysis
mindfulness meditation HRV stress reduction RCT

vitality_ranked_candidates.action_name: Resonance-frequency HRV biofeedback (paced breathing ~6 bpm, 0.1 Hz)

trigger.summary: HRV dropped from 76ms baseline to 61ms avg (low 50ms) in May 2026; resonance-paced breathing has direct evidence for raising vagally-mediated HRV in athletes.

vitality_ranked_candidates.mechanism: Paced breathing at ~0.1 Hz (resonance frequency, ~6 breaths/min) maximizes baroreflex gain and increases functional connectivity within the central autonomic network, raising vagally-mediated HRV indices (RMSSD, HF power). Effect is reproducible within days-weeks of daily practice.
vitality_ranked_candidates.intervention: 10 min/day of paced breathing at 5.5–6 breaths/min (4s in / 6s out) using a guided app (e.g., Eight Sleep / Apple Breathe / dedicated HRV biofeedback). Morning session, before training.

n_of_1.measurable_signal: Eight Sleep HRV 30d avg (ms) and morning RMSSD trend
n_of_1.decision_criterion: Continue if HRV 30d avg recovers to ≥70ms by week 4; iterate (extend to 15 min, add evening session) if 65–70ms; drop if no movement off dip baseline (≤63ms) at week 4.

spareq.s_reason: Active autonomic deflection on a load-bearing axis.
spareq.p_reason: RCT evidence for HRV biofeedback effect in athletes; strong probability of measurable HRV response.
spareq.a_reason: Eight Sleep HRV 30d avg is a direct, daily-available signal in the trial window.
spareq.e_reason: No interaction with active Risk Adjuncts; complementary to alcohol-minimization (both pro-vagal).
spareq.q_reason: Multiple RCTs and recent mechanistic CAN-connectivity work; effect sizes consistent.

vitality_ranked_candidates.risk_interaction: none: orthogonal to all active Risk Adjuncts.

citations.supports: Resonance paced breathing at 0.1 Hz increases functional connectivity within the central autonomic network.
One-week home-based HRV biofeedback improved autonomic and sensorimotor function vs passive relaxation in martial-arts athletes.
Systematic review of biofeedback interventions supports HRV biofeedback for autonomic regulation and stress resilience.

vitality_ranked_candidates.action_name: Replace BJJ with judo or no-gi wrestling club (modified intensity)

trigger.summary: BJJ valve served four functions: high-intensity sympathetic discharge, social-channel via training partners, skill-acquisition novelty, structured combat-sport recovery valve. Judo or no-gi wrestling preserve grappling-pattern, partner-based randori/live drilling, and skill novelty.

vitality_ranked_candidates.mechanism: Grappling-family sports share kinematic and autonomic load patterns: paired live rolling produces sympathetic discharge measurable on wearables; structured belt/skill curricula re-engage skill-acquisition novelty; club training restores the partnered social channel. Judo specifically has documented stress-hormone profiles validating it as a combat-sport recovery analog. If specific BJJ contraindications (e.g., neck/lower-back) drove the sideline, no-gi wrestling or judo nage-waza-light variants can be modulated by limiting takedown volume.
vitality_ranked_candidates.intervention: 2x/week judo or no-gi wrestling session at a local club; first 4 weeks technique + situational drilling only (no full randori), weeks 5–8 add limited live rounds based on HRV next-morning readout.

n_of_1.measurable_signal: Composite: subjective Life slider (0–100), Eight Sleep HRV next-morning post-session, count of named training partners (social-channel proxy)
n_of_1.decision_criterion: Continue if subjective Life ≥80 by week 8 AND post-session HRV recovers to within 5ms of baseline within 24h; iterate (change club / format) if Life 75–80 and HRV recovery >24h; drop if Life unchanged or HRV chronically suppressed >7 days.

spareq.s_reason: Subjective Life below personal baseline; social channel narrowed.
spareq.p_reason: Direct functional analog; high probability of restoring at least 2 of 4 lost functions.
spareq.a_reason: Subjective Life + HRV recovery curve readable within 8 weeks.
spareq.r_reason: Injury risk non-zero (grappling); mitigated by graded ramp; review whatever drove the BJJ sideline first.
spareq.e_reason: No risk-adjunct conflict; complementary to alcohol-minimization (replaces a social-bonding context that often involves drinking).
spareq.q_reason: Mechanistic + cohort-grade only; no head-to-head RCT of grappling-replacement on subjective vitality.

vitality_ranked_candidates.risk_interaction: none: does not contradict any active Risk Adjunct. Note: grappling can increase head-impact exposure; if any prior concussion history, prefer judo ne-waza-only or wrestling.

citations.supports: Judo training and grading produce measurable hormonal/psychological stress-adaptation responses in adult practitioners, supporting grappling as a structured combat-sport stimulus.
Validated test (juji-gatame within Special Judo Fitness Test) demonstrates grappling-fitness measurability across grappling sports — supports cross-modality functional analog.
Wearable-sensor biomechanics work in combat sports establishes that load and sympathetic discharge in grappling can be tracked objectively.

vitality_ranked_candidates.action_name: Sleep-restriction + stimulus-control protocol (CBT-I core elements) targeted at sub-5h nights

trigger.summary: 2–3 sub-5h nights/month is the disruption pocket. CBT-I core elements (stimulus control, sleep restriction, fixed wake time) have the strongest RCT evidence for compressing variability in sleep duration and improving efficiency.

vitality_ranked_candidates.mechanism: Stimulus control + sleep restriction tightens homeostatic sleep pressure and conditions the bed-as-sleep-cue, reducing sleep-onset latency variability and the frequency of short nights. Network meta-analyses rank CBT-I components above pharmacotherapy for sustained improvement.
vitality_ranked_candidates.intervention: Fixed wake time (07:00) 7 days/week, no-bed-unless-sleepy rule, leave-bed-after-20-min-awake rule. Run for 6 weeks. No new pharmacotherapy.

n_of_1.measurable_signal: Eight Sleep: count of sub-5h nights per 4-week window, sleep efficiency %
n_of_1.decision_criterion: Continue if sub-5h nights drop to ≤1/month AND sleep efficiency ≥88% by week 6; iterate (extend, add wind-down protocol) if 2 sub-5h nights and efficiency 85–88%; drop if no change.

spareq.s_reason: Sleep is above-floor on average; pocket disruption is the target.
spareq.p_reason: CBT-I has the strongest RCT effect-size profile of any sleep intervention.
spareq.a_reason: Eight Sleep gives nightly objective signal on the exact target metric.
spareq.r_reason: Behavioral, no pharmacology, no equipment cost.

citations.supports: Network meta-analysis: CBT-I and component psychotherapies most effective and acceptable for adult insomnia, ranking above pharmacotherapy for durability.
RCT meta-analysis confirms CBT-I efficacy for sleep efficiency and onset latency outcomes.
Active midlife/older-adult RCT (ASLEEP) deploying CBT-I infrastructure for prevention of insomnia symptoms.

vitality_ranked_candidates.action_name: Add 2x/week heavy resistance training block (lower body + posterior chain)

trigger.summary: VO2max 54 (~95th %ile) is elite — protect, don't chase. Resistance training at age 47 protects against sarcopenia-driven VO2max decline and adds independent all-cause mortality benefit beyond aerobic training.

vitality_ranked_candidates.mechanism: Resistance training preserves lean mass and neuromuscular function; muscle strength (handgrip and lower-limb) shows dose-response inverse association with all-cause mortality independent of cardiorespiratory fitness. In adults with elite VO2max, the marginal lifespan/healthspan return is greater on the strength axis than on additional aerobic volume.
vitality_ranked_candidates.intervention: 2x/week, 45 min: deadlift or trap-bar variant, split squat, hip hinge, push/pull, in 3–5 rep range, RPE 7–8. Progressive overload tracked.

n_of_1.measurable_signal: 5RM lower-body lift (kg), grip strength (kg), VO2max stability at 12-month CPET retest
n_of_1.decision_criterion: Continue if 5RM up ≥10% by week 8 AND grip strength stable/up; iterate (programming change) if 5RM up <10%; drop if no progress AND HRV chronically suppressed.

spareq.s_reason: Protective rather than corrective; no current strength deficit signaled.
spareq.p_reason: Strong RCT and dose-response cohort evidence for strength → mortality.
spareq.a_reason: 5RM and grip readable in 8 weeks; VO2max preservation is a 12-month signal.
spareq.r_reason: Standard injury risk; mitigated by Jason's existing training base.
spareq.e_reason: No risk-adjunct conflict; complements MTB/snowboard load tolerance.

vitality_ranked_candidates.risk_interaction: reinforces: strength preservation supports long-horizon ATM/cancer-domain healthspan robustness via lean-mass maintenance.

citations.supports: Dose-response association between handgrip strength and all-cause mortality across inflammation strata.
Sarcopenia + impaired respiratory function jointly predict all-cause death — supports preserving both strength and aerobic capacity.
Resistance-training prescription meta-analysis for body composition, muscle strength, and physical performance.

trigger.summary: Passive heat exposure improves vagal tone, lowers resting HR, and (in cohort data) associates inversely with cardiovascular and all-cause mortality at 4+ sessions/week.

vitality_ranked_candidates.mechanism: Repeated heat stress drives heat-shock-protein expression, improves endothelial function, raises plasma volume, and produces parasympathetic rebound that elevates HRV in subsequent days. Best-characterized in Finnish KIHD-cohort dose-response data.
vitality_ranked_candidates.intervention: Finnish-style dry sauna (or comparable home unit) 80–90°C, 15–20 min × 4 sessions/week, ideally post-MTB/strength.

n_of_1.measurable_signal: Eight Sleep HRV 30d avg, resting HR (overnight)
n_of_1.decision_criterion: Continue if HRV 30d avg ≥75ms AND overnight resting HR drops ≥3 bpm by week 8; iterate (frequency or duration) if partial; drop if no movement and tolerance issues.

spareq.s_reason: Adjunctive autonomic support; HRV deflection already addressed by V1.
spareq.a_reason: HRV and resting HR readable in 8 weeks.
spareq.r_reason: Generally safe; hydration and post-workout BP attention required.
spareq.q_reason: Strong observational + recent narrative reviews; few large RCTs.

citations.supports: Comprehensive review of Finnish sauna for healthspan extension; covers autonomic, cardiovascular, and longevity endpoints.
Heat therapy review across health, disease, and disability outcomes.
Sauna bathing in ischemic-heart-disease prevention context — mechanisms and emerging clinical applications.

vitality_ranked_candidates.action_name: Lock evening device-use cutoff 60 min before sleep

trigger.summary: Evening device use prolongs sleep-onset latency and is implicated in short-night clustering; behavioral cutoff is a low-cost adjunct to V3.

vitality_ranked_candidates.mechanism: Evening short-wavelength light + cognitive arousal from devices delays melatonin onset and lengthens sleep-onset latency; removing devices in the hour before bed advances sleep onset and improves objective sleep metrics in crossover RCTs.
vitality_ranked_candidates.intervention: No phone/laptop/TV after 21:30; replace with reading or partner conversation. Phone charges outside bedroom.

n_of_1.measurable_signal: Eight Sleep sleep-onset latency (min), total sleep time
n_of_1.decision_criterion: Continue if onset latency drops ≥5 min AND TST up ≥15 min by week 4; iterate (extend cutoff to 90 min) if partial; drop if no change.

spareq.a_reason: Onset latency is direct nightly Eight Sleep metric.
spareq.e_reason: Compatible with all Risk Adjuncts; possible household friction.
spareq.q_reason: Crossover RCT and systematic review evidence.

citations.supports: Crossover RCT protocol on evening technology use → objective sleep in older adults; method establishes measurable effect on objective sleep.
Systematic review of sleep interventions including evening light/device hygiene as effective behavioral levers.

below_floor.action_name: Cold plunge / cold-water immersion as routine recovery
below_floor.reason: no_evidence_floor: review-level evidence is mechanistic/narrative, RCTs on HRV/wellbeing are small and heterogeneous, and post-strength-training cold plunge can blunt hypertrophy adaptation conflicting with V4.

below_floor.action_name: Wine for 'social vitality' at gym/social channel
below_floor.reason: contradicts_risk_adjunct: alcohol-minimization is an active Risk Adjunct (≤2 drinks/wk or abstain) given ATM het + GI cancer family history.

below_floor.action_name: Additional zone-2 aerobic volume to push VO2max higher
below_floor.reason: already_baseline: VO2max 54 (~95th %ile) is in elite-protect band, not chase band; marginal return on additional aerobic volume is low and competes with V4 strength block for recovery budget.
other: subsumed by V1 (resonance breathing) which has tighter axis-specific evidence and a concrete measurable signal; standalone mindfulness app would duplicate without adding measurable signal.

lost_recovery_valve_notes.function_lost: high-intensity sympathetic discharge (live rolling), social channel (training partners), skill-acquisition novelty (belt/technique progression), structured combat-sport recovery valve (scheduled outlet)
